hero.title: Free SPICE Simulator Online
hero.subtitle: Velxio runs the open-source ngspice engine compiled to WebAssembly. Real device models, full Modified Nodal Analysis, live transient simulation — and you can wire it to real Arduino / ESP32 / RP2040 firmware. Free, no install, no account.
hero.trust: Real ngspice · Free & open-source · Runs 100% in your browser

why.heading: Why Velxio for SPICE?
why.lead: Browser SPICE has historically meant either no SPICE at all (linear approximators) or hosted-only tools that need an account. Velxio runs the real engine, locally, for free.
why.realBody: Same engine as professional EDA workflows, compiled to WebAssembly via eecircuit-engine. No simplified solver — full MNA, full device library.
why.noServerBody: The solver runs in your browser tab. Your circuits never leave your machine. Works offline after the first load.
why.coBody: Arduino / ESP32 / RP2040 / ATtiny85 GPIOs drive SPICE sources, ADCs read solved node voltages. The unique combo no other in-browser tool offers.

devices.heading: Device library
devices.lead: Every component on the canvas maps to a real ngspice device card — not a behavioural stand-in.
devices.passivesBody: Resistors, capacitors (electrolytic / ceramic / polarity-aware), inductors, potentiometers, photoresistors (LDR), photodiodes, NTC thermistors.
devices.diodesBody: 1N4148, 1N4007, 1N5817 / 1N5819 (Schottky), Zener 1N4733 — with proper forward drop, reverse breakdown, and recovery.
devices.bjtBody: 2N2222, 2N3055 (high-current NPN), 2N3906, BC547, BC557 — with cutoff, active region, and saturation behaviour.
devices.mosfetBody: 2N7000 (small-signal N-channel), IRF540 (power N-channel), IRF9540 (power P-channel), FQP27P06 — Level 3 model.
devices.opampBody: LM358, LM741, TL072, LM324, ideal — with realistic saturation rails so output clipping looks correct.
devices.regulatorsBody: 7805, 7812, 7905, LM317 — with dropout voltage. Build a complete power supply on the canvas.
devices.logicBody: 74HC-series DIP-14 packages, basic gates (AND/OR/NAND/NOR/XOR/XNOR/NOT), edge-triggered D/T/JK flip-flops, SR latches.
devices.powerBody: L293D dual H-bridge, optocouplers (4N25, PC817 with CTR), SPDT relays with coil inductance, hysteresis, and flyback diode.
devices.sourcesBody: 9V / AA / coin batteries with realistic ESR, signal generator (sine / square / DC), oscilloscope, voltmeter, ammeter.

faq.heading: Frequently Asked Questions
faq.q1: Is this real ngspice running in my browser?
faq.a1: Yes. Velxio loads ngspice compiled to WebAssembly via the open-source eecircuit-engine. The same SPICE engine that powers professional EDA workflows runs in your browser tab — no server, no install.
faq.q2: What kind of analysis does it run?
faq.a2: Real-time transient analysis. The solver runs Modified Nodal Analysis on every tick (~60 Hz) so you see waveforms evolve as the circuit runs. Non-linear devices iterate to convergence at every step.
faq.q3: Which SPICE device models are supported?
faq.a3: Resistors, capacitors, inductors, ideal voltage and current sources, diodes (1N4148, 1N4007, 1N5817, 1N5819, Zener 1N4733), BJTs (2N2222, 2N3055, 2N3906, BC547, BC557), MOSFETs (2N7000, IRF540, IRF9540, FQP27P06 — Level 3 model), op-amps (LM358, LM741, TL072, LM324, ideal) with saturation rails, linear regulators (7805, 7812, 7905, LM317), optocouplers (4N25, PC817), and relay coils with inductance.
faq.q4: Can I write raw SPICE netlists?
faq.a4: Velxio builds the netlist for you from the schematic — wire components on the canvas and the simulator generates the SPICE cards. You don’t have to handwrite netlists, but the underlying engine is full ngspice and accepts standard device cards.
faq.a5: Yes — multi-channel oscilloscope, voltmeter, ammeter, and signal generator (sine / square / DC, configurable frequency, amplitude, offset). All instruments probe live SPICE node voltages and branch currents.
faq.q6: Can SPICE talk to a microcontroller?
faq.a6: Yes. This is the unique feature. Arduino / ESP32 / RP2040 / ATtiny85 GPIOs drive SPICE voltage sources; ADC inputs read solved node voltages. Build a real firmware-driven analog signal chain end-to-end in one tool.

bottom.title: Run real SPICE — no install, no account
bottom.body: Open the editor and start solving real circuits in seconds.

hero.title: Arduino Mega 2560 Simulator
hero.subtitle: Simulate Arduino Mega 2560 sketches in your browser with full ATmega2560 emulation — 256 KB flash, 54 digital pins, 16 analog inputs, 4 serial ports, and 6 timers. Free and open-source.
hero.trust: Free & open-source · No signup required · Full ATmega2560 emulation

specs.heading: Arduino Mega 2560 — full spec emulation
specs.lead: The ATmega2560 is the most capable AVR8 microcontroller in the Arduino lineup. Velxio emulates its complete peripheral set so every Mega-specific feature works in the simulator.
specs.flashBody: 8× more program storage than Arduino Uno. Run large sketches, complex state machines, and data-heavy applications without storage constraints.
specs.gpioBody: Full PORTA through PORTL emulation. 40 extra I/O pins compared to the Uno — ideal for multiplexed displays, large button matrices, and multi-module projects.
specs.adcBody: 10-bit ADC with 16 channels (A0–A15). Connect multiple sensors, potentiometers, and analog components simultaneously in the simulation.
specs.serialBody: Serial (USART0–3) all emulated. Serial1, Serial2, Serial3 appear in the Serial Monitor — test multi-device serial communication projects.
specs.timersBody: Timer0/1/2/3/4/5. Three extra 16-bit timers (Timer3, Timer4, Timer5) compared to Uno — more PWM outputs and precise timing channels.
specs.pwmBody: 15 pins with hardware PWM support via analogWrite(). Ideal for motor control, servo arrays, LED dimming, and audio output projects.

uses.heading: What the Mega 2560 is used for
uses.lead: The Arduino Mega 2560 is the preferred board for complex makers projects. Simulate them all without hardware.
uses.printerBody: Marlin and RAMPS 1.4 shields run on ATmega2560. Prototype and debug printer control logic in the simulator.
uses.cncBody: GRBL CNC controller and multi-servo robot arms benefit from the Mega's extra I/O pins and timers.
uses.ledBody: Driving 8×8 or 16×16 LED matrices requires many pins. The Mega's 54 digital outputs make it ideal.
uses.sensorBody: 16 analog inputs allow simultaneous reading from temperature, pressure, humidity, and light sensors without multiplexing.

faq.heading: Frequently Asked Questions
faq.a1: The Arduino Mega 2560 is a microcontroller board based on the ATmega2560. It offers 256 KB flash memory, 8 KB SRAM, 54 digital I/O pins (15 PWM), 16 analog inputs, and 4 hardware USART channels — making it the go-to board for large, complex Arduino projects.
faq.q2: Can I simulate Arduino Mega 2560 code in my browser?
faq.a2: Yes. Velxio provides full ATmega2560 AVR8 emulation. Select "Arduino Mega 2560" in the board picker, write your sketch, compile, and simulate — with all 54 digital pins and 16 analog inputs available.
faq.q3: Is the Arduino Mega 2560 emulation accurate?
faq.a3: Velxio uses avr8js for cycle-accurate AVR8 instruction emulation. The ATmega2560 shares the same AVR8 core as the ATmega328P but with extended memory, more ports (PORTA–PORTL), and additional timers (Timer3, Timer4, Timer5) — all emulated.
faq.q4: What additional features does the Mega have over Arduino Uno?
faq.a4: Arduino Mega 2560 adds: 8× more flash (256 KB vs 32 KB), 4× more SRAM (8 KB vs 2 KB), 40 extra digital pins, 10 extra analog inputs, 3 extra hardware serial ports (Serial1, Serial2, Serial3), and 3 extra 16-bit timers (Timer3, Timer4, Timer5).
faq.q5: Does the Mega simulator support multiple Serial ports?
faq.a5: Yes. Serial (USART0), Serial1 (USART1), Serial2 (USART2), and Serial3 (USART3) are all emulated. Each appears in the Serial Monitor tab so you can monitor multi-serial communication projects.

bottom.title: Simulate your Arduino Mega project
bottom.body: Select the Mega 2560 board in the editor and start simulating — full ATmega2560 emulation, no hardware purchase needed.

hero.title: Free Raspberry Pi 3 Simulator
hero.accent: Full Linux · Python · GPIO — In Your Browser
hero.subtitle: Run a full Raspberry Pi 3B with Raspberry Pi OS directly in your browser — ARM Cortex-A53 quad-core emulation via QEMU. Write Python, control GPIO, install packages. No hardware needed.
hero.trust: Free & open-source · QEMU ARM64 · Full Raspberry Pi OS

what.heading: What can you do with the Pi 3 simulator?
what.lead: Velxio emulates a complete Raspberry Pi 3B — not just GPIO pins, but the entire Linux operating system. It's a full computer in your browser.
what.pythonBody: Python 3 pre-installed on Raspberry Pi OS. Run scripts, use pip, import libraries — full Python environment.
what.gpioBody: Use RPi.GPIO or gpiozero to control LEDs, read buttons, drive motors — real GPIO emulation in QEMU.
what.linuxBody: Full bash terminal with apt, nano, git, and all standard Linux tools. Install packages, edit files, run services.
what.armBody: Quad-core 64-bit ARM CPU at 1.2 GHz via QEMU raspi3b machine. Runs the official Raspberry Pi OS image.
what.sdBody: The OS image is pre-loaded. No flashing, no SD card, no power supply — just open the browser and start coding.
what.multiBody: Mix Raspberry Pi 3 with Arduino and ESP32 on the same simulation canvas. Control Arduino from Pi via serial.

compare.heading: Raspberry Pi Pico vs Pi 3 — which to choose?
compare.picoBodyPrefix: Microcontroller. ARM Cortex-M0+ at 133 MHz. Runs Arduino C++ code. No OS, bare-metal. Best for embedded, IoT, sensors.
compare.pi3Body: Full Linux computer. Quad-core at 1.2 GHz. Runs Python, Node.js, bash. Best for automation, servers, GPIO scripting, education.

faq.heading: Frequently Asked Questions
faq.q1: Can I simulate a Raspberry Pi 3 in my browser?
faq.a1: Yes. Velxio emulates a full Raspberry Pi 3B using QEMU raspi3b — ARM Cortex-A53 quad-core at 1.2 GHz running Raspberry Pi OS (Linux). You get a real terminal to run Python, bash, and system commands.
faq.q2: Is this Raspberry Pi simulator free?
faq.a2: Yes. Velxio is 100% free and open-source (GNU AGPLv3). No account, no subscription — simulate Raspberry Pi 3 code in your browser or self-host with Docker.
faq.q3: Can I run Python scripts on the Raspberry Pi simulator?
faq.a3: Yes. The emulated Raspberry Pi 3 runs full Raspberry Pi OS with Python 3 pre-installed. You can run Python scripts, use RPi.GPIO for GPIO control, install pip packages, and more.
faq.q4: What is the difference between Pi Pico and Pi 3 simulation?
faq.a4: Raspberry Pi Pico (RP2040) is a microcontroller — runs Arduino C++ code, no OS. Raspberry Pi 3 is a full Linux computer — runs Python, bash, and system services. Velxio supports both.
faq.q5: Does it support GPIO on Raspberry Pi 3?
faq.a5: Yes. The QEMU-emulated Raspberry Pi 3 supports GPIO via RPi.GPIO and gpiozero Python libraries. Control LEDs, read buttons, and interface with sensors from Python.

bottom.title: Ready to simulate Raspberry Pi 3?
bottom.body: Open the editor, select Raspberry Pi 3, and boot into Linux — right in your browser.

hero.title: Free Raspberry Pi Pico Simulator
hero.subtitle: Write Arduino code for Raspberry Pi Pico and simulate it in your browser — real RP2040 ARM Cortex-M0+ emulation at 133 MHz. 26 GPIO pins, I2C, SPI, UART, ADC. No hardware needed.
hero.trust: Free & open-source · rp2040js emulation · No account needed

boards.heading: Supported RP2040 boards
boards.lead: Both official Raspberry Pi Pico boards are supported with full RP2040 CPU emulation via rp2040js.
boards.picoBody: RP2040 dual-core ARM Cortex-M0+ at 133 MHz. 264 KB SRAM, 2 MB flash. 26 GPIO, 3 ADC, 2× SPI, 2× I2C, 2× UART.
boards.picoWBody: Same RP2040 chip plus Infineon CYW43439 for WiFi 4 and Bluetooth 5.2. Pin-compatible with Pico.
boards.specsBody: Dual-core Cortex-M0+ at 133 MHz, 264 KB SRAM, hardware interpolators, 8 PIO state machines, DMA controller.

examples.heading: Pico example projects
examples.lead: 18+ ready-to-run Raspberry Pi Pico examples — from LED blink to I2C, SPI, and sensor integrations.
examples.blinkBody: Toggle the onboard LED (GP25). The simplest starting point for RP2040.
examples.echoBody: Read from Serial and echo it back. Test UART communication on RP2040.
examples.i2cBody: Scan the I2C bus and report connected devices. Foundation for sensor projects.
examples.adcBody: Read the 12-bit ADC — measure analog voltage, temperature sensor, or potentiometer.
examples.dhtBody: Read temperature and humidity from DHT22. Serial output with formatted readings.
examples.servoBody: Sweep a servo from 0° to 180° using RP2040 PWM. Smooth motion with configurable range.

faq.heading: Frequently Asked Questions
faq.q1: Is this Raspberry Pi Pico simulator free?
faq.a1: Yes. Velxio is completely free and open-source (GNU AGPLv3). Simulate RP2040 code in your browser — no Raspberry Pi hardware needed, no account, no payment.
faq.q2: How does the RP2040 emulation work?
faq.a2: Velxio uses rp2040js — the open-source RP2040 emulator — to simulate the ARM Cortex-M0+ CPU at 133 MHz. Your code is compiled with the official Arduino-Pico core by Earle Philhower.
faq.q3: Does it support Raspberry Pi Pico W?
faq.a3: Yes. Both Raspberry Pi Pico and Pico W are supported. The RP2040 core emulation is identical — WiFi features are planned for a future update.
faq.q4: Can I use Arduino code with Raspberry Pi Pico?
faq.a4: Yes. Velxio compiles your .ino sketch using the arduino-pico core (by Earle Philhower). Standard Arduino functions like Serial, digitalWrite, analogRead, and I2C/SPI work out of the box.
faq.q5: What components work with Pico simulation?
faq.a5: All 48+ components: LEDs, resistors, buttons, DHT22, HC-SR04, servo motors, 7-segment displays, RGB LEDs, NTC sensors, joysticks, and more. Wire them to any of the 26 GPIO pins.

bottom.title: Ready to simulate Raspberry Pi Pico?
bottom.body: Open the editor, select a Pico board, and start coding — no Raspberry Pi hardware required.
